steps.title: Track the two views
steps.content: The passage contrasts the parents' view of the house with the children's view. The mother thinks it is inconvenient because there are no shelves and hardly any cupboards, and the father jokes that the ironwork is like an architect's nightmare. The children, however, have spent two years in London without even a seaside trip, so the country house seems like a fairy palace and paradise to them.

steps.title: Choose the central contrast
steps.content: Choice B reverses the views by claiming the children like the house less than their parents do. Choice C invents shared admiration, even though the parents criticize the house. Choice D contradicts the passage by saying the house is beautiful and well built and that the children miss London. The main idea is the contrast between the parents' complaints and the children's enchantment. The answer is A.
